hook: You already know how to verify a formula for a few specific cases — but how do you prove it works for *every* positive integer, all the way to infinity? A single check, no matter how many cases you try, can never seal that deal on its own. In this unit, you'll discover a remarkably elegant technique that lets one well-structured argument cover infinitely many cases at once.
concept: The unifying idea is that many infinite families of mathematical truths can be proven by anchoring a base case and showing each step forces the next — captured formally by the *Principle of Mathematical Induction* and its close relatives.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to write clean, rigorous induction proofs by establishing a base case and an inductive step to verify statements of the form $P(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$.
Master strong induction, where you assume $P(k)$ holds for *all* $k < n$ simultaneously, unlocking proofs for problems — like prime factorization — where the previous single step isn't enough.

Apply the Well-Ordering Principle — the fact that every nonempty set of positive integers has a least element — to prove results by contradiction and to see why induction is logically equivalent to this foundational property of $\mathbb{N}$.
Extend inductive reasoning beyond the integers to trees, lists, and recursively defined structures, building proofs that follow the shape of the structure itself.
You will use induction to rigorously prove combinatorial identities such as the Binomial Theorem and Pascal's identity, transforming patterns you've observed into airtight mathematical arguments.
